html:
I

It had lately become common chatter at Brightwood HospitalтБатАФbetter known for three hundred miles around Detroit as HudsonтАЩs ClinicтБатАФthat the chief was all but dead on his feet. The whole place buzzed with it.

All the way from the inquisitive solarium on the top floor to the garrulous kitchen in the basement, little groupsтБатАФconvalescents in wheeled chairs, nurses with tardy trays, lean interns on rubber soles, grizzled orderlies trailing damp mopsтБатАФmet to whisper and separated to disseminate the bad news. Doctor Hudson was on the verge of a collapse.

On the verge?тБатАКтБатАж Indeed! One lengthening story had it that on Tuesday he had fainted during an operationтБатАФmighty ticklish piece of business, tooтБатАФwhich young Watson, assisting him, was obliged to complete alone. And the worst of it was that he was back at it again, next morning, carrying on as usual.

An idle tale like that, no matter with what solicitude of loyalty it might be discussed at Brightwood, would deal the institution a staggering wallop once it seeped through the big wrought-iron gates. And the rumour was peculiarly difficult to throttle because, unfortunately, it was true.

Obviously the hour had arrived for desperate measures.

Dr.┬аMalcolm Pyle, shaggy and beetle-browed, next to the chief in seniority, a specialist in abdominal surgery and admiringly spoken of by his colleagues as the best belly man west of the Alleghenies, growled briefly into the ear of blood-and-skin Jennings, a cynical, middle-aged bachelor, who but for his skill as a bacteriologist would have been dropped from the staff, many a time, for his rasping banter and infuriating impudences.

Jennings quickly passed the word to internal-medicine Carter, who presently met eye-ear-nose-and-throat McDermott in the hall and relayed the message.

тАЬOh, yes, IтАЩll come,тАЭ said McDermott uneasily, тАЬbut I donтАЩt relish the idea of a staff meeting without the chief. Looks like treason.тАЭ

тАЬItтАЩs for his own good,тАЭ explained Carter.

тАЬDoubtless; butтБатАКтБатАж he has always been such a straight shooter, himself.тАЭ

тАЬYou tell Aldrich and Watson. IтАЩll see Gram and Harper. I hate it as much as you do, Mac, but we canтАЩt let the chief ruin himself.тАЭ

Seeing that tomorrow was Christmas, and this was Saturday well past the luncheon hour, by the time Pyle had tardily joined them in the superintendentтАЩs office each of the eight, having abandoned whatever manifestation of dignified omniscience constituted his bedside manner, was snappishly impatient to have done with this unpleasant business and be off.

When at length he breezed in, not very convincingly attempting the conciliatory smirk of the belated, Pyle found them glum and fidgetyтБатАФCarter savagely reducing to shavings what remained of a pencil, Aldrich rattling the pages of his engagement book, McDermott meticulously pecking at diminutive bits of lint on his coat sleeve, Watson ostentatiously shaking his watch at his ear, Gram drumming an exasperating tattoo on Nancy AshfordтАЩs desk, and the others pacing about like hungry panthers.

тАЬWell,тАЭ said Pyle, seating them with a sweeping gesture, тАЬyou all know what weтАЩre here for.тАЭ

тАЬAbтАСsoтАСluteтАСly,тАЭ drawled Jennings. тАЬThe old boy must be warned.тАЭ

тАЬAt once!тАЭ snapped Gram.

тАЬIтАЩll say!тАЭ muttered McDermott.

тАЬAnd you, Pyle, are the proper person to do it!тАЭ Anticipating a tempestuous rejoinder, Jennings hastened to defend himself against the impending din by noisily pounding out his pipe on the rim of Mrs.┬аAshfordтАЩs steel waste basket, a performance she watched with sour interest.

тАЬWhere do you get that тАШold boyтАЩ stuff, Jennings?тАЭ demanded Pyle, projecting a fierce, myopic glare at his pestiferous crony. тАЬHeтАЩs not much older than you are.тАЭ

Watson tilted his chair back on its hind legs, cautiously turned his red head in the direction of Carter, seated next him, and slowly closed one eye. This was going to be good.

тАЬDoctor Hudson was forty-six last May,тАЭ quietly volunteered the superintendent, without looking up.

тАЬYou ought to know,тАЭ conceded Jennings drily.

She met his rough insinuation with level, unacknowledging eyes.

тАЬMay twenty-fifth,тАЭ she added.

тАЬThanks so much. That pointтАЩs settled, then. But, all the same, he wasnтАЩt a day under a hundred and forty-six when he slumped out of his operating room, this morning, haggard and shaky.тАЭ

тАЬItтАЩs getting spread about too,тАЭ complained Carter.

тАЬTake it up with him, Doctor Pyle,тАЭ wheedled McDermott. тАЬTell him we all think he needs a vacationтБатАФa long one!тАЭ

Pyle snorted contemptuously and aimed a bushy eyebrow at him.

тАЬHumph! ThatтАЩs good! тАШTell him we all think,тАЩ eh? ItтАЩs a mighty careless, offhand damn that Hudson would give for what we all think! Did you everтБатАКтБатАжтАЭ He pointed a bony finger at the perspiring McDermott, тАЬтАж┬аdid you ever feel moved to offer a few comradely suggestions to Dr.┬аWayne Hudson, relative to the better management of his personal affairs?тАЭ

McDermott rosily hadnтАЩt, and PyleтАЩs dry voice crackled again.

тАЬAs I thought! That explains how, with so little display of emotion, you can advise somebody else to do it. You see, my son,тАЭтБатАФhe dropped his tone of raillery and became sincereтБатАФтАЬweтАЩre dealing here with an odd number. Nobody quite like him in the whole worldтБатАКтБатАж full of funny crotchets. In a psychiatric clinicтБатАФwhich this hospital is going to be, shortly, with the entire staff in straitjacketsтБатАФsome of HudsonтАЩs charming little idiosyncrasies would be brutally referred to as clean-cut psychoses!тАЭ

The silence in Mrs.┬аAshfordтАЩs office was tense. PyleтАЩs regard for the chief was known to be but little short of idolatry. What, indeed, was he preparing to say? Did he actually believe that Hudson was off the rails?

тАЬNow, donтАЩt misunderstand!тАЭ he went on quickly, sensing their amazement. тАЬHudsonтАЩs entitled to all his whimsies. So far as IтАЩm concerned, he has earned the right to his flock of phantoms. He is a genius, and whosoever loveth a genius is out of luck with his devotion except he beareth all things, endureth all things, suffereth long and is kind.тАЭ

тАЬNot like sounding brass,тАЭ interpolated Jennings piously.

тАЬApropos of brass,тАЭ growled Pyle, тАЬbutтБатАФno matterтБатАКтБатАж We all know that the chief is the most important figure in the field of brain surgery on this continent. But he did not come to that distinction by accident. He has toiled like a slave in a mill. His specialty is guaranteed to make a man moody; counts himself lucky if he can hold down his mortality to fifty percent. What kind of a mentality would you haveтАЭтБатАФshifting his attention to Jennings, who grinned, amiablyтБатАФтАЬif you lost half your cases? TheyтАЩd soon have you trussed in a big tub of hot water, feeding you through the nose with a syringe!тАЭ

тАЬYou spoke of the chiefтАЩs psychoses,тАЭ interrupted McDermott, approaching the dangerous word hesitatingly. тАЬDo you mean thatтБатАФliterally?тАЭ

Pyle pursed his lips and nodded slowly.

тАЬYesтБатАФliterally! One of his notionsтБатАФby far the most alarming of his legion, in so far as the present dilemma is affectedтБатАФhas to do with his curious attitude toward fear. He mustnтАЩt be afraid of anything. He must live above fearтБатАФthat is his phrase. You would think, to hear his prattle, that he was a wealthy and neurotic old lady trying to graduate from Theosophy into BahaismтБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

тАЬWhatтАЩs Bahaism?тАЭ inquired Jennings, with pretended naivete.

тАЬHudson believes,тАЭ continued Pyle, disdainful of the annoyance, тАЬthat if a man harbours any sort of fear, no matter how benign and apparently harmless, it percolates through all his thinking, damages his personality, makes him landlord to a ghost. For years, he has been so consistently living above fearтБатАФfear of slumping, fear of the natural penalties of overwork, fear of the neural drain of insomniaтБатАКтБатАж HavenтАЩt you heard him discoursing on the delights of reading in bed to three oтАЩclock?тБатАКтБатАж fear of that little aneurism he knows heтАЩs gotтБатАФthat he has driven himself at full gallop with spurs on his boots and burrs under his saddle, caroling about his freedom, until heтАЩs ready to drop. But whoever cautions him will be warmly damned for his impertinence.тАЭ

Pyle had temporarily run down, and discussion became general. Carter risked suggesting that if the necessary interview with the chief required a gift for impertinence, why not deputize Jennings? Aldrich said it was no time for kidding. McDermott again nominated Pyle. Gram shouted, тАЬOf course!тАЭ They pushed back their chairs. Pyle brought both big hands down on his knees with a resounding slap, rose with a groan, and sourly promised heтАЩd have a go at it.

тАЬAttaboy!тАЭ commended Jennings paternally. тАЬWatson will do your stitches, afterwards. He has been getting some uncommonly nice cosmetic values, lately, with his scars; eh, Watty?тАЭ

The disorder incident to adjournment spared Watson the chagrin of listening to the threatened report of JenningsтАЩ eavesdropping, an hour earlier, on the dulcet cooing of a recently discharged patient, back to tender her gratitude. Emboldened by his rescue, he dispassionately told Jennings to go to hell, much to the latterтАЩs faunlike satisfaction, and the staff evaporated.

тАЬLetтАЩs go and eat,тАЭ said Pyle.

As they turned the corner in the corridor, Jennings slipped his hand under PyleтАЩs elbow and muttered, тАЬYou know damned well what ails the chief, and so do I. ItтАЩs the girl!тАЭ

тАЬJoyce, you mean?тАЭ

тАЬWho else?тАЭ Jennings buttoned his overcoat collar high about his throat and thrust his shoulder against the big front door and an eighty-mile gale. тАЬCertainly, I mean Joyce. SheтАЩs running wild, and heтАЩs worrying his heart out and his head off!тАЭ

тАЬMaybe so,тАЭ Pyle picked his footing carefully on the snowy steps. тАЬBut I donтАЩt believe itтАЩs very good cricket for us to analyze his family affairs.тАЭ

тАЬNonsense! WeтАЩre quite past the time for indulging in any knightly restraints. HudsonтАЩs in danger of shooting his reputation to bits. Incidentally, it will give the whole clinic a black eye when the news spreads. If the chief is off his feed because heтАЩs fretting about his girl, then itтАЩs high time we talked candidly about her. SheтАЩs a silly little ass, if you ask my opinion!тАЭ

тАЬWell, you wonтАЩt be asked for your opinion. And itтАЩs no good coming at it in that mood. She may be, as you say, a silly little ass; but sheтАЩs HudsonтАЩs deity!тАЭ

Jennings motioned him to climb into the coup├й and fumbled in his pockets for his keys.

тАЬShe wasnтАЩt behaving much like a deityтБатАФunless Bacchus, perhapsтБатАФthe last time I saw her.тАЭ

тАЬWhere was that?тАЭ

тАЬAt the Tuileries, about a month ago, with a party of eight or ten noisy roisterers, in the general custody of that good-for-nothing young MerrickтБатАФyou know, old Nick MerrickтАЩs carousing grandson. Believe me, they were well oiled.тАЭ

тАЬDid youтБатАФdid she recognize you?тАЭ

тАЬOh, quite so! Came fluttering over to our table to speak to me!тАЭ

тАЬHumph! She must have been pickled! I thought she was getting on, all right, at a girlsтАЩ school in WashingtonтБатАКтБатАж DidnтАЩt know she was home.тАЭ

Jennings warmed his engine noisily, and threw in the clutch.

тАЬMaybe she was sacked.тАЭ

Pyle made some hopeless noises deep in his throat.

тАЬToo bad about old MerrickтБатАКтБатАж Salt of the earth; finest of the fine. HeтАЩs had more than his share of trouble. Did you ever know Clif?тАЭ

тАЬNo. He was dead. But IтАЩve heard of him. A bum, wasnтАЩt he?тАЭ

тАЬThat describes him; and this orphan of his seems to be headed in the same direction.тАЭ

тАЬOrphan? I thought this boyтАЩs mother was livingтБатАФParis or somewhere.тАЭ

тАЬOh yes, sheтАЩs living; but the boyтАЩs an orphan, for all that. Born an orphan!тАЭ Pyle briefly reviewed the Merrick saga.

тАЬPerhaps,тАЭ suggested Jennings, as they rolled into the club garage, тАЬyou might have a chat with old Merrick, if heтАЩs such a good sort, and tell him his whelp is a contaminating influence to our girl.тАЭ

тАЬPfff!тАЭ Pyle led the way to the elevator.

тАЬWell, if that proposalтАЩs no good, why donтАЩt you go manfully to the young lady herself and inform her that sheтАЩs driving her eminent parent crazy? Put it up to her as a matter of good sportsmanship.тАЭ

тАЬNo,тАЭ objected Pyle, hooking his glasses athwart his nose to inspect the menu, тАЬshe would only air her indignation to her father. And he likes people to mind their own businessтБатАФas youтАЩve discovered on two or three occasions. He keeps his own counsel like a clam, and doesnтАЩt thank anybody for crashing into his affairs, no matter how benevolent may be the motiveтБатАКтБатАж It would be quite useless, anyway. Joyce canтАЩt help the way sheтАЩs made. She is a biological throwback to her maternal grandfather. You never knew him. He was just putting the finishing touches to his career as a periodical sot when I arrived in this town, fresh from school. Cummings was the best all тАЩround surgeon and the hardest all тАЩround drinker in the state of Michigan for twenty years; one of these three-days-soused and three-weeks-sober drunkards. This girl evidently carries an overplus of the old chapтАЩs chromosomes.тАЭ

тАЬYou mean she is a dipsomaniac?тАЭ

тАЬWellтБатАФthatтАЩs a nasty word. LetтАЩs just say sheтАЩs erratic. Ever since she was a little tot, she has been a storm centre. Sweetest thing in the world when she wants to be. And then all hell breaks loose and Hudson has to plead with the teachers to take her back. Oh, sheтАЩs given him an exciting life; no doubt of that! And lately itтАЩs booze!тАЭ

тАЬHudson knows about that part of it, of course!тАЭ

тАЬI presume so. How could he help it? She makes no secret of it. At all events, sheтАЩs no hypocrite.тАЭ

Jennings sighed.

тАЬRather unfortunate she has this one embarrassing virtue; isnтАЩt it? But, that being the case, I dare say sheтАЩll have to go to the devil at her own speed. We must persuade Hudson, however, to clear out and take a long leave of absence. He can take her along. Lay it on with a heavy hand, Pyle. Be utterly ruthless! Tell him it affects us all. That ought to fetch him. I never knew anybody quite so sensitive to the welfare of other people. Save that card for the last trick: tell him if he doesnтАЩt clear out, for a while, he will do up the rest of us!тАЭ

For the first half hour of their conference, which was held in the chiefтАЩs office the following Tuesday, Pyle stubbornly held out for a trip around the world, Joyce to accompany her father. Indeed, the idea had seemed so good that he had armed himself with a portfolio of attractive cruise literature. He had even made out an intriguing itineraryтБатАФHawaii, Tahiti, ukelelesтБатАФPyle was a confirmed landlubber with a dangerously suppressed desire to lie on his back, pleasantly jingled, under a trans-equatorial palm, listening to the soft vowels of grown-up children unspoiled by civilizationтБатАФthe Mediterranean countries, six months of hobnobbing with brain specialists in Germany. The latter item had been included as a particularly tempting bait. Hudson had often declared he meant to do that some day.

The chief listened preoccupiedly; tried to seem grateful; tried to seem interested; but as Pyle rumbled on with his sales-talk the big man grew restless, refilled his fountain-pen, rearranged his papers in neater piles, had much difficulty hunting a matchbox. Then he shook his head, smiling.

No, much as he appreciated PyleтАЩs friendly concern, he wasnтАЩt going around the world; not just now. Of course he had been sticking at it too steadily. Lately he had had it on his mind to build a little shack in some out of the way place, not too far off, and put in there from Friday afternoon to Tuesday morning, at least in decent weather, tramp, fish, botanize, read light novels, sleep, live the simple life. He would begin plans on such a place at once. Spring would be along soon.

тАЬAndтБатАФmeantime?тАЭ persisted Pyle, gnawing at the tip of his uptilted little goatee.

Hudson rose, slammed a drawer shut with a bang, swung a leg over the corner of his desk, folded his arms tightly, and faced his counsellor with a mysterious grin.

тАЬMeantime?тБатАКтБатАж Pyle, I hope this wonтАЩt knock you cold. IтАЩm going down to Philadelphia, week after next, to marry my daughterтАЩs school friend, Miss Helen Brent.тАЭ

PyleтАЩs eyes and mouth comically registered such stunned amazement that the Hudson grin widened.

тАЬAnd then the three of us will be spending a couple of months in Europe. IтАЩve arranged with Leighton to come over from the university and take care of such head cases as Watson canтАЩt handle. WatsonтАЩs a good man; bright future. Oddly enough, I was on the point of asking you in to talk this over when you said you wished to see me.тАЭ

Pyle bit off the end of a fresh cigar and mumbled felicitations, not yet sufficiently recovered to pretend enthusiasm.

тАЬDoubtless you think me a fool, Pyle.тАЭ

Hudson took a turn up and down the room, giving his colleague an opportunity to deny it if he wished. Pyle puffed meditatively.

тАЬSeventeen years a widower,тАЭ mused Hudson, half to himself. He paused at the far corner to straighten a disordered shelf of books.

тАЬA man accumulates a lot of habits in seventeen years.тАЭ He returned to his desk-chair. тАЬSounds like the wedding of January and June, eh?тАЭ

Had Jennings been in PyleтАЩs place, his eyes would have twinkled as he replied, тАЬJanuary! What! You? January? Nonsense, Chief! Not a day over October, at the farthest!тАЭ

Pyle smiled wanly, and shifted his cigar to the other corner of his mouth.

тАЬI came by this valuable new friendship early last year when Miss Brent was made Junior advisor to my Joyce.тАЭ

Something of sympathetic comradeship in PyleтАЩs reviving interest, now that he was partially coming to, encouraged Hudson to toss aside what remained of his reticence and tell it all.

To begin withтБатАФMiss Brent was an orphan; parents reputable Virginians; most interesting French background on her motherтАЩs side; same kind of blood that the guillotine spilt in 1789тБатАКтБатАж тАЬQuite pronouncedly Gallic, she isтБатАФat least in appearance.тАЭ

Jennings, had he been there, would have been audacious enough to suggest, slyly chuckling, тАЬOhтБатАФin that case we should amend June to July!тАЭ Then he would have watched the chiefтАЩs face intently.

But Pyle, who had no traffic with psychoanalysis, attached no significance whatever to the fact that the young ladyтАЩs probable temperament was somewhat on the chiefтАЩs mind.

тАЬAbout Thanksgiving,тАЭ Hudson was saying, тАЬMiss Brent, after a brief encounter with influenza, left the school and spent a few days at home. No sooner was she gone than Joyce slipped out, one night; attended a party, down in the city; defied some house rules as to hours; flicked all her classes next day; stormed until the shingles rattled when they rebuked her; and, in short, contrived to get herself suspended, notwithstanding that her recordтБатАФthanks to Miss BrentтАЩs influenceтБатАФhad been quite above reproach ever since she matriculated, a year ago last September.тАЭ

The story went forward rather jerkily. Hudson was not given to confiding his perplexities to anybody. Pyle discreetly remained silent.

тАЬWellтБатАФshe came home and plunged immediately into a series of hectic affairs; out every night; in bed most of the day; nervous, testy, unreasonable. I canтАЩt tell you, Pyle, how thoroughly it did me inтБатАКтБатАж SheтАЩs all I have you know.

тАЬAt my witтАЩs end, I suggested that she invite Miss Brent up to visit us through the holidays. Twice before had she been our guest for a few days, and I had seen something of her on my occasional visits to Washington. Believe me when I tell you that this charming girl was no more than across our threshold last week, than Joyce was another creature, poised, gracious, lovableтБатАФa lady!тАЭ

He paused to take his bearings before going further; impelled to explain how the swift movement of events, that first evening at dinner, amply accounted for his decision to ask Helen to marry him; reluctant, even in the interest of plausibility and self-defence, to give words to the memory of that occasion. It had all been so natural; so unimpeachably right; so precisely as it ought to be! He had remarkedтБатАФperhaps a bit more ardently than he intended, for his heart was fullтБатАФhow happy it had made himтБатАФand JoyceтБатАФthat she had come. тАЬI donтАЩt see how we can ever let you go!тАЭ he had said; to which Joyce had added impetuously, тАЬWhy need she ever go? SheтАЩs happier here than anywhere else; arenтАЩt you, darling?тАЭ

Pyle recrossed his legs and cleared his throat to remind the chief that he was still present.

тАЬAs a matter of fact, Miss Brent is certain to be happier with us than she was at home. Since childhood, she has lived with an uncle, her fatherтАЩs elder brother, an irascible, penurious, not very successful old lawyer. There are no women in the family. And I have reason to suspect that her cousin, Montgomery Brent, is a bit of a rake, though she has idealized him out of all proportion; calls him тАШBrother Monty,тАЩ thinks him vastly misunderstood by his father and everybody elseтБатАКтБатАж that kind of a girl, PyleтБатАКтБатАж espouses the cause of homeless cats, underdogs, misunderstood cousins, my flighty, wilful JoyceтБатАКтБатАж and nowтБатАФthank GodтБатАФshe has promised to join forces with me! I think sheтАЩs making something of a mission of it, Pyle. I was quite willing to wait until she had finished school in June; had some serious misgivings, indeed, about that; but she dismissed the thought lightly. If I needed her, I needed her now, she saidтБатАКтБатАж I hope to God it works out!тАЭ

Pyle said he believed it would; moved to the edge of his chair; looked at his watch; asked if this was a secret.

Hudson stroked his jaw, his eyes averted.

тАЬI donтАЩt object to their knowingтБатАКтБатАж LetтАЩs consider it sufficient, for the present, that IтАЩm going to Europe with my daughter.тАЭ He mopped his broad forehead vigorously. тАЬThe rest of it they can learn in due time. Report to Aldrich and Carter and the others that IтАЩm off on a vacation.тАЭ

тАЬAny special word for Mrs.┬аAshford, Chief?тАЭ Pyle paused with his hand on the doorknob.

Hudson thrust his hands deeply into his trouser pockets and walked to the window, staring out. тАЬIтАЩll tell her myself, Pyle,тАЭ he answered, without turning.

Doctor Hudson named his isolated retreat Flintridge. It was quite remote from the beaten trail of travel. A mere acre had been tamed to serve the cottage for which his hasty sketches, before leaving, were elaborated and executed in his absence by his loyal friend, Fred Ferguson, the best architect in town.

It was an inhospitable bit of country, thereabouts. Sheer cliffs, descending abruptly to the black water (a long flight of wooden steps led to the little boathouse and adjacent wharf) had discouraged such colonization as had long since developed the western shore, two miles distant. Deformed pines clawed the rocks, sighing of their thirst in summer, shrieking of nakedness in winter.

Almost from the first, Flintridge never knew certainly, for there was no telephone, when its master would appear for a weekend. It anticipated, made forecasts, baked ineffable angel food cakes, caught vast quantities of minnows for bait, and held itself in instant readiness to welcome the big man with the ruddy face (just a shade too ruddy, any heart diagnostician could have told him), silver-white hair, grey eyes with deep crows-feet, and expressive hands eloquent of highly developed dexterity.

When and if he came, it would be on Saturday, late afternoon. Once only had he brought Joyce and HelenтБатАФstrangers, passing them, presumed they were both his daughtersтБатАФbut that was merely temporizing with his promise to seek a retreat. And he now needed days off, if ever; for his young wifeтАЩs gregarious disposition and charming hospitality had multiplied his social obligations in the city.

How easily she had adjusted herself to his moods! How proud he was of her, not quite so much for her exotic beauty, as because of her exquisiteness of personal taste and the tact with which she met the rather exacting problems of fitting neatly and quickly into his circle of mature acquaintances. It delighted him that she chose the right word, wore the right costume, intuitively knew how to manage a dinner-party without seeming concerned as to what misadventures might have occurred in the kitchen. Yes; the affair was тАЬworking outтАЭтБатАФhow often he used that phrase!тБатАФimmeasurably better than he had dared hope.

Even the women liked her! They had accepted her on approval at first; but when it became evident that she had no intention of taking on airs because their grizzled spouses fluttered about her with the broad compliments privileged to fifty addressing twenty-five, they admitted she was a dear.

But, however pleasant it was for Hudson to note his wifeтАЩs growing popularity, certified to by the increasing volume of their social activities, his new duties contributed little to the reinvigoration of that fatigued aorta which had worried Pyle.

тАЬThe chiefтАЩs in better fettleтБатАФthink?тАЭ said Jennings.

тАЬTemporarily,тАЭ conceded Pyle. тАЬBut you donтАЩt mend an aneurism with late dinners, three a week. IтАЩm afraid heтАЩll crash, one of these days.тАЭ

Not infrequently some visiting colleagueтБатАФfor Brightwood now not only attracted patients from afar but had become a mecca for the ambitious in the field of brain surgeryтБатАФwould be driven out into the country to rusticate for a day or two. They seemed singularly alike, these brain-tinkers from otherwhere; moody, abstracted men, in their late forties and early fifties, most of them; seldom smiling, ungifted with small talk, not unusually inclined to be somewhat gruff. Hudson preferred to hold conferences with them at the lake, for their conversation would be tiresomely technical. And anyhow, men who trafficked daily with Death could not be expected to enliven a house party.

A devoted pair of middle-aged twins served as caretakers at Flintridge. What time Perry Ruggles, of the stiff knee, hairy throat, and Airedale disposition, was not tinkering the boat engine with greasy wrenches or trolling in and out of season for bass, he was teaching little patches of apathetic soil to take a maternal interest in iris and petunias. On Saturdays about five oтАЩclock, he would put on his other coat and limp down to the gate that admitted from the narrow ridge road; and, having opened it, would flick little stones off the driveway with his good foot.

Martha, his buxom sister, wrought ingenious quilts, concealed from the taciturn Perry the vandalisms of an impertinent, bottle-fed fawn; was silly over a pair of tame pheasants whose capacity for requiting her affection was as feeble as her need was great; scratched her plump arms gathering early berries in anticipation of some high moment when her pie would be approved with a slow wink, of which the learned guest, profoundly discoursing of surgical mysteries to his celebrated host, would be entirely unaware.

On Saturdays, about four-thirty, having again made sure she had laid out the doctorтАЩs pyjamas on the bed, and turning the vase of roses on his chiffonier a little more to the advantage of the tallest, Martha would take her stand before the window in the sun-parlour, her knuckles pressed hard against her pretty teeth, devoutly praying for a swirl of yellow dust and a flash of glittering nickel at the bend of the ridge road, visible through an open lane of dwarf spruce.

At the sound of gravel crunching under heavy tires, she would dash to the door and fling it open, always hopingтБатАФand hating herself for itтБатАФthat the doctor had come alone or, if not alone, accompanied by another man. She had been uneasy, abashed, and awkward in the presence of young Mrs.┬аHudson, whose beauty had stirred remembrance of a certain pre-Christmas shopping excursion when she was nineтБатАКтБатАж There had been a French doll, so beautiful it had made MarthaтАЩs little throat ache with longing. Her wistful eyes had gushed sudden tears, and she had put out a hand, tentatively.

тАЬNo, dear,тАЭ her mother had cautioned. тАЬYou may look at her, but you mustnтАЩt touch.тАЭ

On the broad mantel in the тАЬgun roomтАЭ (there had been a bit of chaffing about the тАЬgun room,тАЭ seeing there was only one gun in it among all the miscellaneous instruments of sportтБатАФgolf clubs, fishing tackle, and the like) an impressive row of silver cups testified that Wayne Hudson was no less expert at play than with the more important implements of surgery.

It was a frequent remark of his intimates that Hudson possessed an almost uncanny capacity for projecting the sensitiveness of his cognitive fingers to the very tips of whatever tools he chose to manipulate. There were nerves in his niblick, in his casting-rod, in his scalpel.

тАЬA lucky devil!тАЭ bystanders used to remark when he had successfully made a long putt upgrade on a sporty green.

тАЬAn uncommonly good guesser!тАЭ his confr├иres agreed occasionally, when some quite daring prognosisтБатАФprobably defining the exact location of a brain tumour on such cryptic evidence as the arc of an eyebrow, the twitch of a lip, the posture of a hand in repose, or the interjection of an unbecoming phrase into casual conversationтБатАФwas verified.

Among the trophies on the mantelтБатАФwhose inscriptions always amazed a visiting colleague, marvelling at his distinguished hostтАЩs diversity of proficienciesтБатАФthere was a tarnished triple-handled aquatic prize won by Doctor Hudson when, in the early days of his internship, he had taken a First in a mile swim.

тАЬStill swim?тАЭ

тАЬRegularly.тАЭ

тАЬEnjoy it?тАЭ

тАЬWellтБатАФitтАЩs good for me.тАЭ

тАЬKeeps your weight down?тАЭ

тАЬPerhaps. But, in any event, itтАЩs good for me.тАЭ

Some time in the course of his visit, the visitor would rag his athletic host upon the excess of his prudence; for the most conspicuous article of furniture in the тАЬgun roomтАЭ was an elaborate but not very decorative inhalator of the super-type used by lifesavers at busy wharves and crowded beaches, equipped with nickelled oxygen tanks and a complication of mechanical mysteries.

тАЬWhatтАЩs that thing?тАЭ

Hudson would tell him, briefly, brusquely.

тАЬWhat do you want with it?тАЭ

тАЬOh, somebody might fall in. The waterтАЩs deep out here.тАЭ

It was clear enough to the guest, if he ventured to press his queries, that Doctor Hudson did not enjoy any talk about aquatics. The guest found himself wondering why. Perry Ruggles could have explained, had he been disposed. There had once been a very anxious hour at Flintridge, down on the narrow pier. Not even Martha knew. The next time he had come out, Doctor Hudson had brought the inhalator, and had explained its use to the terrified Perry who thereafter stood in dread of the thing. It became a grim spectre that haunted his life. Some day, he suspected, he would be obliged to experiment with it. The responsibility constituted a steady, remorseless threat that tortured him and kept him awake nights. Some sharp, man-to-man candour had been handed to the surgeon, that afternoon, by his uncouth caretaker. It had been a long time since anybody had called Wayne Hudson a fool to his face. He accepted the degree with dignity.

тАЬPerhaps I am, Perry,тАЭ he replied soberly. тАЬYou probably wouldnтАЩt know. But, however that may be, the thing youтАЩre to keep in mind is that this top valve controls the oxygen; and if you have occasion to use it, donтАЩt get excited and forget.тАЭ

Not a few men of importance to the surgical world resident in widely-spaced cities, recalled having had brief and somewhat disquieting conversations about swimming, while guests at Flintridge, when, one Sunday morning in early August, they read the front-page dispatch which reported that Doctor Wayne Hudson, widely known brain surgeon of Detroit, had drowned, the late afternoon before, near his summer place on Lake Saginack.

At their breakfast table in Seattle, Doctor Herman Bliss read the shocking headlines to his wife, and when she had commented sympathetically, he added:

тАЬNot only very sad, my dear, but very strange!тАЭ

Pressed for explanation, he reviewed for her the incidents of a visit he had paid to his friend at the lake cottage, and the heavy constraint which had fallen upon their conversation when he had made some inquiries about his hostтАЩs enjoyment of the water.

тАЬDo you suppose,тАЭ conjectured Mrs.┬аBliss, тАЬthat there could have lurked in his mind some vague mirage of the fate that waited for him?тАЭ

Her husband pursed his lips and shook his head. тАЬI donтАЩt take much stock in such theories,тАЭ he declared, almost too vehemently to be convincing.

тАЬBut you told me once that Doctor Hudson was тАШprescientтАЩ!тАЭ

тАЬOnly a form of speech, Grace. Nobody is prescient. However, Hudson was extraordinarily sentient; psychic to an uncommon degree.тАЭ

тАЬBut why did he persist in swimming,тАЭ inquired Mrs.┬аBliss, тАЬif he was afraid of it?тАЭ

тАЬFor that very reason, unquestionably. I never knew a man so impatient of normal peopleтАЩs timidities, or more passionately eager to make himself independent of fear. Doubtless this was the one thing that gave him anxiety, and he was resolved to master it.тАЭ

тАЬButтБатАФby the same logic,тАЭ objected Mrs.┬аBliss, тАЬhe might have jumped off a precipice, if he found himself afraid of that.тАЭ

тАЬNot quite the same thing! Here was something he had been able to do with ease, skill and safety. Now, for some reason, he had suddenly become afraid of it. An experience of cramp, perhapsтБатАКтБатАж Might happen again. The fear filtered through his thinkingтБатАКтБатАж Had prided himself on living in complete mental libertyтБатАКтБатАж Knew now that he was housing a dread! So long as he gave that phobia the hospitality of his mind, he would be, by that much, no longer his own man; so he decided to go to the mat with his antagonist. I fancy that explains.тАЭ

The newspaper account further detailed that by a singular coincidence the inhalator which Doctor Hudson owned, and kept at his cottage, was in use on the other side of the little lake at the exact moment of his own tragic need of it.

A few hundred yards off shore, near his grandfatherтАЩs estate, тАЬWindymere,тАЭ young Robert Merrick, alone in his sailboat, had been knocked unconscious by a jibbing boom and pushed into the water.

тАЬMust have been drunk,тАЭ indignantly commented Doctor Bliss. тАЬThings like that donтАЩt often happen to sober people.тАЭ

Excited bathers, informed that there was an inhalator at the Hudson cottage, had rushed a speedboat across for it, and after an hourтАЩs heroic exertion, were successful in restoring the young man to partial consciousness. It was said that he would undoubtedly recover.

тАЬUnquestionably! He would!тАЭ growled Bliss.

It was believed, said the dispatch, that had the inhalator been immediately available and promptly applied, Doctor HudsonтАЩs life might have been saved. The caretaker, Perry Ruggles, observing the evident distress of his employer, had rowed quickly to the spot, dived for him, dragged his limp body into the boat. Desperately, Ruggles had set forth with his unconscious passenger towards the Windymere beach, and had rowed until his strength failed. Small craft, attracted by his signals, hurried to him; found him huddled over the lifeless body of Doctor Hudson, weeping hysterically, while the little boat drifted in the middle of the lake.

тАЬNever saw such doglike devotion as old PerryтАЩs. I suppose he went in after him, clothes and all; bad leg too.тАЭ

Robert Merrick, the paper continued to explain, was the only son of the late Clifford Merrick and Mrs.┬аMaxine Merrick, now resident in Paris. He had only that day returned from an extended visit with his mother, having gone abroad immediately after finishing at the State University in the midyear senior class. He was further identified as the grandson of Nicholas J. Merrick, retired founder and large stockholder of the Axion Motor Corporation, with whom he made his home at Windymere.

тАЬHope this youngster will be able to realize how valuable a person he is,тАЭ said Doctor Bliss, putting down the paper, тАЬnow that he has had his life handed back to him at such a price!тАЭ

There was still another coincidence connected with this event. The village physician, suspecting that MerrickтАЩs head injury might be in need of more skilful examination than he could give it, had sent him in a swift ambulance to Brightwood Hospital. At that moment he did not know that the man who had made Brightwood famous for its brain surgery would be unable to see his young patient.

тАЬWhat do you suppose the boy said,тАЭ speculated Mrs.┬аBliss, тАЬwhen he learned what it had cost to save his life?тАЭ

тАЬWell,тАЭ reflected the doctor glumly, тАЬfrom my own observation of the type of young cub whose father is dead, whose mother lives in Paris, whose doting grandfather is a retired millionaire, and who gets himself bumped off his boat by a boom in broad daylight, I should suppose he just scratched a match on the head of his bed and mumbled, тАШWhadda yuh know about that!тАЩтАКтАЭ
